gameMetadata.title: The Illusion of Success
gameMetadata.description: A 4-year university journey navigating the tension between societal expectations and authentic purpose.

nodes.text: Welcome to your freshman year. The next four years will dictate your future. Before the journey begins, you must declare your major. This choice will define the expectations society places upon you.
nodes.quote: "The perceived security of the status quo is a dangerous illusion."

choices.text: Engineering - Chase the tech boom and build the infrastructure of tomorrow.
Humanities - Dive into the pursuit of history, narrative, and societal critique.

nodes.text: Your introductory engineering class is designed to weed people out. It feels like everyone around you is ahead of you. There is immense pressure to stand out.

choices.text: Cheat on the final project by copying an upperclassman's design.
Coast through your classes and decide to wait another year before applying to internships.
Build a small, open-source tool for a local charity at the expense of your time and grades.

nodes.text: You scored a prestigious interview, but the company builds addictive social media algorithms. Your peers are jealous.

choices.text: Ace the interview. Optimize the algorithm for maximum user retention.
Show up unprepared and bomb it intentionally to escape the pressure.
Use the interview to question their ethics. You get blacklisted.

nodes.text: Your open-source project was mocked by your peers for lacking ambition and not generating money. You're offered a chance to join one of the best hackathon teams on campus, but their project is a sketchy crypto exchange.

choices.text: Join the team for the prize money and resume boost - the LinkedIn post after winning will feel great.
Skip the hackathon because you want to relax for the weekend.
Form a rival team to build a decentralized voting app. You lose the competition because your app doesn't have a "use case".

nodes.text: Coasting is catching up to you. The impostor syndrome is killing your self-esteem. A professor offers you a spot in a research lab, but it requires long and uncredited hours.

choices.text: Take the lab spot and steal a grad student's data to publish first.
Respectfully decline because you don't want any new responsibilities.
Take the lab spot, but insist on open-sourcing the data for public good.

nodes.text: You're quickly becoming one of the best engineers on campus. You're asked to build scrapes private user data for a club's marketing campaign. They tell you it's "standard practice".

choices.text: Write the program perfectly to receive a grant from the university.
Ask someone on fiverr to do it for you then take the credit.
Refuse and report the privacy violation, but lose your grant and reputation.

nodes.text: Every day feels the same. Today, you get grouped with highly motivated students for a major project. They tell you their idea is to build a chatbot that humanizes AI-generated essays.
nodes.quote: "A society in which consumption has to be artificially stimulated is a society founded on trash and waste." - John de Graaf

choices.text: Agree to start the venture, it could be profitable.
Let them do all the work while you just put your name on it.
Refuse and build your own project that helps students write better without cheating.

nodes.text: You are socially isolated but morally grounded. A local grassroots organization asks you to build a system to track corporate pollution, but it pays nothing and will take all your time.

choices.text: Abandon them, it's a waste of time anyway. Take a lucrative freelance gig to work on missle defense systems.
Tell them you are too busy and go back to sleep. Your homework is more important anyway.
Commit to the pollution tracker. Your grades tank, but the tool exposes a major leak.

nodes.text: The 'Affluenza' has set in. You are obsessed with your starting salary. A defense contractor offers you a massive signing bonus to optimize drone targeting systems.

choices.text: Sign the contract. You'll be set for life.
Ghost the recruiter - it's too tough of a decision.
Reject it, you take an offer from a local eco-friendly startup instead.

nodes.text: The engineering career fair is happening on campus, and you don't know if your resume is good enough to get you a job.

choices.text: Lie on your resume to get yourself a high-paying job.
Skip the career fair and hang out with some friends.
Go to the fair, but only hand resumes to local non-profits that pay minimum wage.

nodes.text: You're broke and your resume looks 'unconventional' to say the least. You discover a critical flaw in your university's cybersecurity system that exposes students' info.

choices.text: Blackmail the university for some money and a referral at a high-paying firm.
Just ignore it. Their issue has nothing to do with you.
Try to fix it, but you'll have to stay inside and drop everything else for a few days.

nodes.text: The burn out is real. You can't get enough caffeine or social validation. For your senior capstone, your team wants to build an application that could potentially automate millions of jobs.

choices.text: Take the lead on this one. The potential profitability is mouthwatering.
Let your team do all the work while you focus on job hunting.
Decide to build something that helps people get jobs instead.

nodes.text: Nothing is interesting anymore. You get assigned to maintain legacy code for the university library system. It's boring work that leaves you wanting more.

choices.text: Change the codebase to charge students insanely high late fees to improve your own metrics.
Just make sure everything is working.
After getting approval from the library, make academic journals free for students.

nodes.text: No one seems to like you, but that doesn't stop you. You need to use a mssive amount of power for your capstone. The 'normal' way is to use GPUs that consume horrific amounts of energy and water.

choices.text: Take the easy route and use these resources. You need to graduate.
Make your project more basic to avoid the issue altogether.
Redesign the entire project to work on low-power embedded microcontrollers.

nodes.text: It's finals season and you have a massive job offer waiting. If your GPA drops it gets rescinded. You discover the professor's exam key is circulating on a private server...

choices.text: Download the key. Everyone else is so you might as well. Besides, you need that job.
Ignore the key, but don't bother studying anyway.
Bring this to the attention to the professor. Everyone in your class is calling you a snitch.

nodes.text: Final exams hit and you're barely passing. A recruiter from a major defense firm reaches out, looking for new interns.

choices.text: Jump at this chance, it's the break you need..
You don't know how to respond, so you delete your draft.
Criticize their company practices and reach out to exciting startups instead.

nodes.text: Final exams. You're respected by a few, but completely ignored by the corporate recruiters. A startup offers you a large amount of equity to develop an app that takes advantage of gig workers.

choices.text: You're tired of being broke, miserable, and lonely. Accept the offer and get to building.
Decline the offer, but stop working on your own positive projects.
Decline the offer and create an open-source application for delivery drivers.

nodes.text: You're in the final stretch. You are 'successful.' You have the job, the money, the clout. But you feel completely empty. A massive tech conference invites you to speak on Innovation.

choices.text: Deliver a standard speech generated by ChatGPT, sprinkle in some buzzwords along the way.
Cancel at the last minute due to stage fright.
Use the stage to publicly denounce the immoral practices of many firms in the industry. Preach change.

nodes.text: You're completely checked out. You have no job lined up and no motivation. Your friend offers you a referral at a company that makes homeownership difficult for the average person.

choices.text: Take the referral because you need the money.
Say you'll apply, but never get around to it.
Refuse the referral and ask this friend to work on an app to help students with you.

nodes.text: You're barely scraping by financially, but you feel great on the inside. You get nominated for a community service award, but attending means missing your final exam review.

choices.text: You need to go to this review session, so you skip the award completely.
Sleep through both the review and the award ceremony.
Attend the ceremony. You're unprepared for the exam, but you find the place you truly belong.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. You are signing the final paperwork for a life you aren't sure you want. A junior asks you for advice on how to succeed.

choices.text: Tell them to put their head down, grind hard, and chase the bag no matter what.
Tell them it doesn't matter, it's all meaningless anyway.
Tell them to find their true self, to not let the expectations of society confine them to a narrow path.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. You coasted through four years and have nothing to show for it, but a piece of paper and a whole lot of regret.

choices.text: Panic and accept a soulless data-entry job just to have an income.
Accept your fate. Do nothing like you've been doing.
Decide it's not too late. Volunteer to teach STEM at a local middle school.

nodes.text: Graduation is tomorrow. You don't have a high-paying job lined up, but your tools are helping thousands. A corporate recruiter makes you a massive offer.

choices.text: You're tired of fighting, so you take the money and finally sell out.
Ignore the email and stare at the ceiling.
Delete the email, you don't need a cushy job to feel fulfilled.

nodes.text: Semester 1. Your introductory humanities seminar is electric with ideas, but your career counselor is already steering you toward marketing and corporate strategy. 'Literature and history won't pay your loans,' she says. 'Learn to sell.'
nodes.quote: "The worker becomes all the poorer the more wealth he produces." - Karl Marx

choices.text: Plagiarize a viral essay for your portfolio workshop to stand out from the class.
Disengage from workshops entirely. Watch other students from a safe distance.
Start a free zine covering stories from your campus community that mainstream media ignores.

nodes.text: Semester 2. Your sharp writing catches the eye of a PR firm. They want you to craft a campaign burying negative press about a corporation involved in a wage theft lawsuit. The work is well-paying.
nodes.quote: "The Puritan wanted to work in calling; we are forced to do so." - Max Weber

choices.text: Write the campaign. You learn to make exploitation sound like generosity.
Take the money but do sloppy, half-hearted work so the campaign fails.
Decline the contract. Write an exposé on the firm's tactics and publish it yourself.

nodes.text: Semester 2. Your zine gains a small but dedicated readership. A student media organization offers to absorb it — but only if you accept corporate advertising and stop covering campus labor organizing.
nodes.quote: "Human progress never rolls in on wheels of inevitability." - Martin Luther King Jr.

choices.text: Accept the merger. The zine reaches more people even if the stories change.
Kill the zine. You're tired of fighting for it. Retreat into your coursework.
Keep the zine independent. You lose the platform boost, but double down on the labor coverage.

nodes.text: Semester 2. You are withdrawing further. A screenwriting professor spots your raw talent and offers to connect you with an entertainment agent — but the agent only handles content for a streaming platform known for exploitative labor practices.
nodes.quote: "He who has a why to live for can bear almost any how." - Viktor Frankl

choices.text: Pursue the agent. Your script gets optioned. You don't ask where the money comes from.
Agree to meet the agent, then cancel the day before. Classic.
Decline and channel the script into an independent short film about gig workers, shot on your phone.

nodes.text: Semester 3. You are developing a reputation as a skilled writer and researcher for hire. A political consulting firm pays top dollar to write targeted disinformation designed to suppress voter turnout in communities of color.
nodes.quote: "The sad truth is that most evil is done by people who never make up their minds to be good or evil." - Hannah Arendt

choices.text: Write the content. It's technically legal. You are very good at it.
Take the deposit and write something vague and unusable. Keep the money.
Refuse and write a public thread documenting the firm's request in detail.

nodes.text: Semester 3. Your independent journalism is reaching people. A prominent investigative news outlet wants your story on campus labor organizing — but their editor demands you name a source who asked to remain anonymous.
nodes.quote: "We are caught in an inescapable network of mutuality." - Martin Luther King Jr.

choices.text: Name the source. The story runs. The source loses their job.
Agree to name the source, then kill the story at the last minute. Burn the bridge.
Protect the source. Pull the story and publish a shorter, source-free version independently.

nodes.text: Semester 4. Junior Year. You have clout in media circles. A major news network offers a prestigious internship — writing chyrons and copy that frames poverty as a personal failing, never structural. It's the style guide.
nodes.quote: "A society in which consumption has to be artificially stimulated... is a society founded on trash and waste." - John de Graaf

choices.text: Accept. You absorb the framing until it feels like your own voice.
Accept the internship but produce nothing, paralyzed between compliance and conscience.
Decline and instead embed yourself with a local community newspaper covering housing displacement.

nodes.text: Semester 4. Junior Year. Your independent work has a loyal following. You uncover evidence that the university is ghost-writing positive editorials about itself and submitting them to the student newspaper under fake names.
nodes.quote: "There is something fundamentally wrong with treating the Earth as if it were a business in liquidation." - Herman Daly (via Suzuki)

choices.text: Sit on the evidence. The university controls your scholarship review.
Do nothing with it. Save the files in a folder you never open.
Publish the exposé. Risk your scholarship. The university issues a public apology.

nodes.text: Semester 5. You are polished and employable. For your senior project, your advisor pushes you toward a 'safe' academic analysis. You have a different story you need to tell — about your own family's history of displacement.
nodes.quote: "The worker is related to the product of his labor as to an alien object." - Karl Marx

choices.text: Write the safe project. It gets top marks. Your own story stays buried.
Start your personal project, then abandon it halfway. Turn in something unfinished.
Write the true story of your family's displacement. Your advisor gives you a C. It is the most important thing you have ever written.

nodes.text: Semester 5. Your housing displacement coverage gets traction. A real estate developer threatens legal action to silence your reporting. A media law clinic can defend you for free — but the case will consume your academic life.
nodes.quote: "We are showering radioactive fallout... to kill insects, we are destroying the birds and the fishes." - Rachel Carson

choices.text: Retract the story. Issue a carefully worded apology. Move on.
Go silent. Delete your accounts. Disappear into your coursework.
Fight the legal threat. The case takes a year but the reporting stands. Tenant groups rally around you.

nodes.text: Semester 6. Graduation looms. A content farm offers a full-time job writing SEO articles designed to spread health misinformation under the guise of wellness content. The salary is extraordinary.
nodes.quote: "The banality of evil... the terrifyingly normal adherence to the system." - Hannah Arendt

choices.text: Take the job. You write a hundred articles undermining public health. You buy a nice apartment.
Take the job but write articles so bad they never publish them. Collect paychecks and feel hollow.
Refuse. Spend the semester writing a reader's guide to identifying health misinformation, published for free.

nodes.text: Semester 6. Your legal fight made you known in press freedom circles but toxic to mainstream media employers. A small nonprofit journal offers you a staff position — the pay is poverty level, but the work is fully independent.
nodes.quote: "When we speak of nature, we are speaking of ourselves." - David Suzuki

choices.text: Turn it down. You need a livable wage. Take a corporate content strategy role instead.
Accept the job offer, then spend the first month staring at a blank document, overwhelmed.
Accept and throw yourself into the work. Your first piece changes a local election.

nodes.text: Semester 7. You are polished and well-compensated. A junior classmate has written a devastating, important piece about sexual harassment in the department. No publication will touch it. They ask you to use your connections to help them publish it.
nodes.quote: "Everything can be taken from a man but one thing: the last of the human freedoms—to choose one's attitude." - Viktor Frankl

choices.text: Decline. One of the people named in the piece is a powerful contact of yours.
Promise to help, then do nothing. Ghost them.
Use every connection you have to get it published. Burn the powerful contact. The piece changes policy.

nodes.text: Semester 7. Your work is honest and your integrity intact. A large national publication offers you a staff job — on the condition that you sign away the rights to all your previous independent work, including your family displacement story.
nodes.quote: "Action on behalf of life transforms. Because the relationship between self and the world is reciprocal." - Robin Wall Kimmerer

choices.text: Sign the rights away. The platform is worth it. Your family's story is now owned by a media conglomerate.
Neither sign nor decline. Let the offer sit unread for months.
Refuse the rights clause. Walk away from the offer. Publish your family story as an open-access piece that spreads widely.

nodes.text: Semester 8. Graduation. You are a skilled writer with a comfortable career ahead of you. A first-year student shows you their raw, urgent, true piece of writing and asks if they should pursue this path.
nodes.quote: "The illusion of security is the greatest danger." - Game Thesis

choices.text: Tell them to learn SEO, build a brand, and monetize. Kill the urgency before it kills them.
Tell them you don't know. Shrug. Walk to your own graduation.
Tell them their story is the most important thing in the world and someone needs to tell it. Make sure it's them.

nodes.text: Semester 8. Graduation. You have no staff job lined up, but your work has mattered. A media conglomerate makes one final offer: a high salary to run their 'social impact' content vertical, which will give ethical cover to their other exploitative divisions.
nodes.quote: "We are now faced with the fact that tomorrow is today. We are confronted with the fierce urgency of now." - Martin Luther King Jr.

choices.text: Accept. You'll try to do real work from the inside. You know how this ends.
Leave the email unread. Procrastinate until the offer expires.
Decline. Start an independent media cooperative with three other graduating students who feel the same.

nodes.text: Semester 4. Avoidance Node. (Routing back to main paths).

choices.text: Snap out of it and conform.
Keep avoiding.
Wake up and rebel.

nodes.text: Semester 5. Avoidance Node. (Routing back to main paths).

choices.text: Snap out of it and conform.
Keep avoiding.
Wake up and rebel.

nodes.text: Semester 6. Avoidance Node. (Routing back to main paths).

choices.text: Snap out of it and conform.
Keep avoiding.
Wake up and rebel.

nodes.text: Semester 7. Avoidance Node. (Routing back to main paths).

choices.text: Snap out of it and conform.
Keep avoiding.
Wake up and rebel.

nodes.text: Semester 8. Graduation is tomorrow. You spent four years with the gift of narrative and said almost nothing with it.

choices.text: Accept your fate.

epilogueLogic.description: When the game reaches 'epilogue_trigger', the UI drops the visible stats entirely. The game reads the final hidden stats to deliver the conclusion.

conditions.title: The Iron Cage Achieved
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: You have everything society told you to want. You are incredibly wealthy and highly respected. But your work has systematically harmed others, automated away jobs, and extracted resources without giving back. You stare at your massive bank account, completely alienated from your own life. The dangerous illusion worked.

conditions.title: Authentic Resistance
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: Your resume is a mess. You never made the 30-under-30 list. But the systems you built empower people. You rejected the status quo, and while society punished you with lower pay and less prestige, you sleep soundly. You broke the illusion, and in doing so, you found out what actually matters.

conditions.title: The Apathetic Drift
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: You avoided doing evil, but you also avoided doing good. You coasted through the system, neither conforming deeply nor rebelling meaningfully. You escaped the worst of the corporate machine, but you failed to find your authentic purpose.

conditions.title: The Mixed Reality
conditions.text: 10 Years Later: You compromised. You took some of the money, and you tried to do some good on the side. You are comfortable, but the tension between the societal illusion and your inner authenticity remains a daily battle.
